text: BIDDESTONE (St. Nicholas and St. Peter), a parish in Wilts, 4 m. W of Chippenham. Area 2,530 acres. Pop. 452.
BIDDICK (NORTH), a township in the p. of Whitburn, co. of Durham. Pop. 371.
BIDDICK (South), a township in the p. of Houghton-le-Spring, 6 m. WSW of Sunderland. Area 280 acres. Pop. 74.
BIDDISHAM, a parish in Somersetshire, 3 m. W by S of Axbridge. Area 510 acres. Pop. 145.
BIDDLE, a lake in the state of Missouri, U. S., in the Rocky mountains, under the parallel of 2° 34' N, the source of the Bighorn river.
BIDDLESDON, a parish in Bucks, 4 m. NE of Brackley. Area 1,630 acres. Pop. 169.
BIDDLESTON, a township in Allenton p., in Northumberland, 15 m. W by S of Alnwick. Pop. 140.
BIDDULPH, a parish in Stafford, 6 m. WNW of Leek. Area 5,530 acres. Pop. 2,314.

BIDDULPH'S ISLANDS, a group in the Persian gulf, first described by Captain Biddulph of the sloop Hesper. They are 4 in number, and lie between N lat. 27° 41' and 27° 55', and E long. 49° 26' and 49° 31'. They are mere sand-banks and rocks, elevated from 4 to 6 ft. above the surface of the sea. Their only productions are turtles and birds' eggs.

BIDEFORD, or BIDDEFORD, a seaport in the co. of Devon, 9 m. SW of Barnstaple; in N lat. 51° 2', W long. 4° 3'; occupying both sides of the river Torridge, which, joining the Taw, falls into Barnstaple Ford. bay. The river is crossed by a bridge of 24 arches, and 677 ft. in length, built in the 14th cent. There is a fine quay here, 1,200 ft. in length, at which ships of 500 tons burden can take in and unload cargoes. lages. A great quantity of earthen-ware is manufactured and exported to Wales; and there is an extensive manufacture of woollen stuffs and carpeting. Shipsof-war and vessels of inferior size are built here. B. is a place of considerable trade, exporting sail-cloth, cordage, grain, and oak-bark; and its commerce continued to increase for some time after that of Barnstaple had declined. The number of sailing vessels belonging to the port in 1845, was 64 under 50 tons burden each, and 89 above it. The total tonnage being 12,616 tons. The tonnage which entered inwards coastwise in 1845 was 24,706 tons; from the colonies 2,179 tons; and from foreign ports 790 tons. B. is an incorporated town, and is governed by its own magistrates. Pop. of the p., which is co-extensive with the borough, in 1801, 2,987; in 1831, 4,846; in 1841, 5,211.
BIDER. See BEEDER.
BIDFORD, a parish in Warwickshire, 4 m. S by E of Alcester, on the N bank of the Avon. Area 3,240 acres. Pop. 1,567.
BIDGULL, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Irak, 5 m. N of Cashan.
BIDISSUR, a town of Hindostan, in Orissa, 28 m. WSW of Cuttack.
BIDJANAGOR. See BIJANAGUR. BIE
BIDJIE, a large town of Upper Guinea, in N lat. 6° 45′, 20 m. S of Jenna.
BIDLIS. See Bedles.
BIDNUR. See BEDNORE.

BIDOUZE, a river of France, in the dep. of the Lower Pyrenees, which rises in the Pyrenees, 16 m. SW of Mauléon, and flows into the Adour near Bayonne, after a course of 65 m. It is navigable to the village of Came, a distance of 16 m. from the sea.
BIDOUZE (Pic DE), a lofty mountain on the S face of the Pyrenean chain, in N lat. 42° 41'. Alt. 10,014 ft.

BIDSCHOW, or Bydzow, an administrative subdivision of Austria, in Bohemia; bounded on the N by the Riesengebirge, which separates it from Silesia; on the E by the circles of Königgrætz and Chrudim; on the S by the circles of Chrudim, Czaslau, and Kaurzim; and on the W by that of Jung Bunzlau. Its area is 44.9 German or 953 English sq. m. The Elbe rises in the N part of this district, and skirts the E side of the circle. Its principal tributary in the circle is the Czidima. The N part is rugged and mountainous; in the S there are extensive tracts of level and fertile ground. Large forests exist in several quarters. In 1837, of 391,680 jochs of productive land, 206,700 were in culture, 112,956 under wood, 31,241 in pasturage, 32,009 in meadow land, and 7,871 in gardens and orchards. The agricultural productions are wheat, barley, oats, potatoes, lint, and some oleaginous plants. Iron is wrought in the mountains connected with the Riesengebirge. Thread, linen, paper, and wood-ware, form the chief articles of manufacture. Pop. in 1833, 251,414; in 1837, 258,557, nearly all Tshekhes. There are 9 towns, 19 market-towns, and 612 villages in the prov. Its cap. is Gitschin.
BIDSCHOW (NEU), or Nowy Bydzow in Bohemian, a town in the above circle, situated in the Cydlina, 18 m. SSE of Gitschin. Pop. 3,863, of whom 460 are Jews. It was the cap. of the circle till 1784.
BIDSTONE, a parish in Cheshire, 7 m. NNE of Great Neston. Area 3,090 acres. Pop. in 1841, 1,013, of whom 291 were in the township of B. cum
BIDUMI, a district of Syria, bounded on the W by Egypt, and on the E and S by Arabia. It is nearly desert, and contains only a few straggling vil-
BIEBER. See BIBER.
BIECZ, a town of Austria, in Galicia, in the circle of Lemberg, on the Ropa, 9 m. W of Jaslo. Pop. 1,912.—The Polish historian, Martin Kromer, was born here.

BIEDENKOPF, or BIDENKAPP, a town of the duchy of Hesse-Darmstadt, in the principality of Upper Hesse, 12 m. NW of Marburg, on the l. bank of the Lahn, at an alt. of 1,220 ft. above sea-level. It has iron-forges and founderies, and woollen manufactories. Pop. 3,180.
BIEF-D'ETOY, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Doubs, com. of Charmauvillers, 12 m. from Montbeliard.
BIEL. See BIENNE.
BIELA, a market-town of Bohemia, in the circle of Czaslau, 6 m. E of Deutsch Brod.—Also a town of Spain, in the subdiv. of Saragossa, in Aragon, 40 m. NW of Huesca.
BIELAU, or BIELE, or BILLAU, a small river of Prussian Silesia, which issues from the hill of Lautersberg, in the principality of Neisse, and flows into the Neisse near Glatz.

BIELAU (LANGEN), a town of Prussian Silesia, in the circle of Reichenbach. It is divided into Middle, Upper, Lower, and New B.; and has a pop. of nearly 8,000. The greater number are weavers, who manufacture serge, fustian, muslin, and other stuffs.

BIELAYA, a river of Asiatic Russia, in the gov. of Orenburg, rising at an alt. of 1,901 Parisian ft. above sea-level, in the Ural chain, to the NW of Vershni- Uralsk, and flowing into the Kuma on the l. bank, after a course of 650 m., in which it passes Sterlitamak and Ufa. Its waters are turbid, and of a whitish colour.
BIELBSKOI, a town of Siberia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, 40 m. S of Eniseisk.
BIELEF, or BIELER, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Tula, on the l. bank of the Oka.

BIELEFELD, a circle and town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine. The circle has an area of 95 sq. m., and is watered by the Lietter and the Aa. The town, in N lat. 52° 1', at the foot of the Sparenberg, is the cap. of the co. of Ravensberg in Westphalia. It had 6,550 inhabitants in 1817; and 6,097 in 1837; and flourishing manufactures of leather, soap, woollen stuffs, linen, thread, and Meerschaum pipes, with excellent bleaching-grounds. The chief trade is in linen, which used at one time to bring yearly into circulation here half a million dollars.

BIELGOROD, or BIELOGOROD, an old town in European Russia, the cap. of a circle in the gov. of Kursk, and the see of an archbishop. It is about 4 m. in circuit, and contains 1,800 houses. Pop. in 1838, 10,318. It has an active trade, partly in foreign goods, and partly in productions of the country, such as honey, wax, tallow, hemp, leather, and soap.
BIELINY, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Sandomir, obwod of Opatow. Pop. 1,000.
BIELITSA (Novo), a town of Russia, in the gov. of Mohilew, on the Soje river, 140 m. S of Mohilew. Pop. 2,000.

BIELITZ, a duchy of Austrian Silesia, in the circle of Teschin, containing about 60 sq. m., and 10,000 inhabitants.—The cap., of the same name, lies on the l. bank of the Biela, at the NW base of the Carpathians, and is united to Biala in Galicia by a massive stone bridge. It was totally destroyed by fire in 1808; but has been rebuilt, and now contains 6,500 inhabitants. It is an industrious and commercial place; and has numerous manufactories of leather, cloth, linen, &c. The principal market for its manufactures is Pesth. The ponderous and lofty castle of Prince Sulkowsky imparts to the town an imposing appearance.
BIELITZ (ALT), a town of Austrian Silesia, in the circle of Teschin, near Bielitz. Pop. 1,400.

BIELLA, a mountainous province of Piedmont, in the division of Turin, watered by the Cervo and the Elvo, affluents of the Sosia. It contains 78 com., and had a pop. of 94,958 in 1821. Corn, rice, and hemp are the chief agricultural productions. BIELLA, BIELA, or BIOGLIO, a small but populous town of Piedmont, the cap. of the above district. It lies partly on the summit, and partly on the declivity of a hill, near the rivers Cervo and Aurena, 36 m. NNE of Turin. It is divided into Upper and Lower B.; and contains 4 churches and 4 monasteries. Pop. 8,250.
BIELOI, an island of Asiatic Russia, in the sea of Karskoe, 70 m. in circuit.

BIELOI, a town of European Russia, in the gov. of Smolensk, on the river Obsheha. Pop. 3,476. It is the cap. of a circle, and carries on an extensive trade with Riga. BIELO-OZERO, [i. e. 'White lake,'] a large lake of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Novgorod, between the district of Kirilov and Bielozersk. It is upwards of 84 m. in circumf., and is of considerable depth. It receives twenty-six small rivers, and discharges itself by the Sheksna, a tributary of the BIE Volga, of which it may also be regarded as an expansion.
BIELOPAUTZE, a town of Turkey in Europe, in Albania, in the pashalik and 65 m. N of Scutari, on the l. bank of the Moracca. The surrounding district is extremely wild.

BIELOPOLIE, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Kharkov, in the district and 35 m. NW of Soumy, on the r. bank of the Vira. It is surrounded by ramparts and ditches, and contains 8 churches. Pop. 10,000. The distillation of brandy forms the most important industrial occupation of the inhabitants. Several fairs are held here in the year.—Also a town in the gov. and 87 m. SW of Kiev, in the district of Maknovka.
BIELORIETZK, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Orenburg and district of Verkho-Uralsk, on the l. bank of the Biela, 100 m. SE of Oufa. It has extensive iron-works.
BIELOSTOK. See BIALYSTOCK.
BIELOTSERKOVKA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 32 m. W of Poltova, in the district and 30 m. ESE of Khorol, on the l. bank of the Psiol.
BIELOVEJ, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 58 m. S of Grodno, 20 m. WNW of Proujang.

BIELOVODSK, or Belovodsk, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Voronez, in the district and 25 m. E of Starobielsk, on the Derkoul. An extensive establishment for rearing horses, founded by Peter the Great, exists here.

BIELOZERSK, a town of Russia in Europe, the cap. of the district of the same name, in the gov. of Novgorod, on the S bank of the Bielo-Ozero. Pop. 3,000. It conducts an active trade in corn, pitch, tallow, and cattle. B. is of considerable antiquity, and was formerly the cap. of a principality. It was taken and destroyed by the Lithuanians in 1678.— The district is to a great extent hilly, and is watered by upwards of 50 streams. A great portion of its surface is covered with forests. The soil is tolerably fertile; but its production in grain is inadequate for the local consumption. Iron, in considerable quantities, is extracted from the marshes. It contains 1 city, 1 town, 126 parishes, and a pop. of 3,977.—Also a town of Russia in Asia, in the gov. and 200 m. SSW of Tobolsk, in the district and 30 m. NNE of Kurgan, on the l. bank of the Tobol.
BIELSA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Aragon, 42 m. NE of Huesca, on the Cinca. Iron is abundant, and silver mines formerly existed in the environs.

BIELSK, a town of Poland, in the gov. and 10 m. N of Plock, and 58 m. NW of Warsaw. It contains 4 churches.—Also a town of Russia in Europe, cap. of the district of the same name, in the gov. and 65 m. SSW of Grodno, and 28 m. S of Bialstok. It is well-built and paved, and contains 2 Catholic churches and a handsome custom-house. Pop. 2,400, consisting chiefly of Greeks. Jews are excluded from the town. The district is watered by the Narev and Nurzek; it is generally fertile, and produces grain in considerable quantities. Wood is extremely scarce.
BIELSKAMESKOI, a fortress of Asiatic Russia, on the E side of the Irtisch, 12 m. SE of Semipolatnoi.
BIENDORF, a village in the duchy of Anhalt, on the Ziethe. Pop. 150.
BIENGEN, a village in Baden, in the Upper Rhine circle, SW of Freyburg. Pop. 904.

BIENNE, or BIEL, a well-built town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Bern, on the river Suze, or Scheuss, 16 m. W of Basle, and half-a-mile from the N extremity of the lake of Bienne, between Solothurn and Neufchatel. Pop. 4,248, chiefly Protestants. It is still surrounded by its ancient walls. The lake of B. is lined on both sides with productive vineyards. from which, and the cultivation of grain, the inhabitants derive their chief subsistence. The town, however, contains iron forges, wire manufactories, tanneries, and manufactories of cottons and chintz. It has a good gymnasium, and a public library. The environs afford fine views of the Bernese alps and Mont Blanc.—B. anciently belonged to the bishop of Basle. During the occupation of this part of Switzerland by the French, B. belonged with its territory, to the dep. of Haut Rhin, in which it was the head of a canton. At the congress of Vienna in 1815, the whole was annexed to the Swiss canton of Bern.

BIENNE (LAKE OF), or BIELER-SEE, a fine sheet of water in the Swiss cant. of Bern, about 10 m. in length, by 2 m. in breadth, formed by an expansion of the Zihl or Thiele, which links it on the SW to the lake of Neufchatel, and issuing from its NE extremity, at the castle of Nydau, conveys its superfluous waters into the Aar. It is 1,338 ft. above sealevel; 178 ft. above the level of the lake of Geneva; and 8 ft. below that of Neufchatel. Its greatest depth is 217 ft. Its scenery is nowise bold, but possesses much soft quiet beauty. Rousseau, however, is enthusiastic in his praises of this lake, on an islet in which he took refuge during his fancied persecution.

BIENNE, a river of France, an affluent of the Ain, which it joins on the l. bank, after a course of 45 m. A considerable quantity of willows and small timber is annually floated down this river for market at Lyons.
BIENSEWIDA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Estremadura, subdivision of Chinchilla, between the Guadalimar and the Guadarmena rivers.
BIENTINA, a small town of Tuscany, in the territory and 13 m. E of Pisa, on the Canale Imperiale, which joins the Arno on the r. bank. Pop. 2,200.
BIENVILLE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, cant. of Chevillon, on the Marne, 7 m. NE of Vassy. Pop. 456. There are iron-furnaces here.

BIENVILLERS-AU-BOIS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Pas-de-Calais, cant. of Pas. Pop. 1,182. BIEQUES, VIEQUE, BORIQUEEN, OF CRAB ISLAND, one of the Virgin group in the W Indies, in about 18° 9' N lat., and 65½° W long. Its E extremity forms nearly an equilateral triangle with the Danish islands of Santa Cruz and St. Thomas. It is about 25 m. long, and 5 m. broad; but has no permanent population, though resorted to by vessels of all nations for supplies of wood and water. Yet Flinter represents it as being so fertile as to be capable of maintaining a dense population. On the NE side it is low and level; but towards the SW and W it rises into hills and sandy hummocks.
BIERCZA, a town and seigniory of Austria, in the prov. of Galicia, circle and 25 m. ENE of Sanok.
BIERE, a parish and v. of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 6 m. NW of Aubonne. Pop. 992.

BIERETZ, a charming little watering-place on the coast of France, in the dep. of Basses-Pyrenees. It stands upon mounts and hollows, on a piece of rugged ground which forms a promontory about 100 yards long, and 50 yards across, with two sides dipping perpendicularly into the sea, and the others sloping down to the rocks and caverns. In front is the bay of Biscay; on the E, the coast of France stretching away in an immense low white curve; while to the W the bold outline of the Spanish coast reaches, in another vast segment, as far as Bilbao.

BIERFLIET, or BIERVLIET, a small town of Holland, on an island of the same name, in the W Scheldt, in the prov. of Zealand, between Sluys and Axel, 20 m. N of Ghent; 12 m. ENE of Sluys. Pop. 1,350. The island has been submerged by inundations of the sea in 1377, 1404, 1444, and 1477. It was I. BIE in former times a place of strength, but the fortifications were demolished in 1688.
BIERGE, a village of Belgium, in S Brabant, NE of Genappe. Pop. 665.
BIERINGEN, a village of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Black forest, bailiwick and 7 m. E of Horb. Pop. 620.
BIERKAGER, a village of Norway, in the district of S Trondheim, on the Orkel river.
BIERLEY (NORTH), a township in the p. of Bradford, W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. SE of Bradford. Area 3,090 acres. Pop. 9,512.

BIERNE', or BIERNES, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, arrond. of Chateau-Goutier.—The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 8,879.—The com., 9 m. E of Chateau-Goutier, had a pop. of 1,021 in 1846.
BIERSET, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. and arrond. of Liege. Pop. 365.
BIERSTEIN, or BIRSTEIN, a village of Kur-Hessen, in the district of Hanau, on the Vogelsberge. Pop. 965.
BIERT, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Ariege, cant. of Massat. Pop. 1,717.
BIERTON-WITH-BROUGHTON, a parish in Bucks, 2 m. NE of Aylesbury. Area 2,470 acres. Pop. 605.

BIERZO, a district of Spain, to the W of Astorga, encircled by branches of the Asturian mountains, viz., by the Puerto del Rabanal, the Cruz de Ferro, and Foncebadon, on the E; the Sierra de Sanabria, the Sierra de Cabrera, and the Montes Aquilianos, on the S; and the Puerto del Cebrero, Puerto del Courel, and Puerto del Aguiar, on the W. From N to S, computing from summit to summit, this amphitheatre measures about 42 m.; and from E to W, about 36 m. All its waters, collected into the Sil, pass into the Val de Orras, in Galicia, through a narrow gorge; and if that opening were closed the whole B. would be formed into a lake. Wine, corn, pulse, flax, and fruits, are abundantly produced in this region; and the olive grows wild upon the hills. The name is supposed to be a corruption of the Bergidum Flavium of Ptolemy, which stood at Castro de la Ventosa. The B., from the multitude of its sanctuaries, the holiness of its hermitages, the number of its anchorites and monks, and the richness of its hagiology, "is the Thebais of Spain." Southey has given an interesting account of it in his Letters from Spain.

BIESBOSCH, a large expansion of the estuary of the Maas, between Dort and Gertruydenburg in Holland, which was formed by the bursting of the dykes of the Waal and Maas on the 19th November, 1421, when 72 villages with their inhabitants—said to have been 100,000 in number-were suddenly overwhelmed and destroyed. The only remains of this once fruitful tract are a few islands. It is supplied with water from the Maas, and gives rise to the Hollands-diep, or Haringsvliet.—It is half covered with reeds, whence its name which signifies 'the Reed wood or forest.'
BIESCAS, a town of Spain, in Aragon, in the prov. and 25 m. N of Huesca, on the l. bank of the Gallego river.
BIESE, a small river of Prussian Saxony, in the Old mark of Brandenburg, circle of Stendal, which, under the name of the Aland, falls into the Elbe near Schnakenburg.
BIESENBERG, a village in the archduchy of Austria, on the Danube, near Korn-Neuburg.
BIESENTHAL, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Brandenburg, circle of Potsdam, on the Finow, 20 m. NE of Berlin. Pop. 1,240. It contains a castle; and is the head of a bailiwick of 13 villages. 3 A
BIESHEIM, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haut-Rhin, cant. of Neuf-Brisach. Pop. 1,767.
BIESIGHEIM. See BESIGHEIM.
BIESINGEN, a village of Baden, in the Lake circle, bailiwick of Radolfzde, on the Rhine. Pop. 589.
BIESLES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Marne, cant. of Nogent-le-Roi. Pop. 1,090.

BIESME, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, 18 m. SW of Namur. Pop. 890. There are iron-works here.-Also a river of France, an affluent of the Aisne, which it joins near Viennele-Chateau, in the dep. of Marne, after a course of 20 m.
BIETIGHEIM, a village of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, 5 m. NNE of Rastadt. Pop. 1,209.
BIEUZE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Baud, 8 m. SW of Pontivy. Pop. 1,534.
BIEVE'NE, a commune and town of Belgium, in the dep. of Hainault, 40 m. E by N of Tournay. Pop. 3,270.

BIE'VRE, a river of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, an affluent of the Savre, which it joins on the r. bank after a course of 40 m.—Also an affluent of the Seine, which rises to the SW of Versailles, and after a NE course of 20 m., discharges itself into the Seine, on the l. bank, in the city of Paris. It supplies numerous large manufacturing establishments; and sometimes receives the name of the Riviere-des- Gobelins, from that celebrated manufactory.
BIEVRES, a commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-et-Oise, cant. of Palaiseau, 5 m. SE of Versailles.
BIEZUN, a town of Poland, in the district of Plock, circle of Mlawa, on the Ukra. Pop. 1,600.
BIFARA, a village of Sicily, in the district of Caltanisetta, N of Alicata.
BIFERNO, a river of Naples, in the county of Molise, in the Capitanata, which falls into the gulf of Venice, 5 m. SE of Termoli, after a course from SW to NE of 70 m.
BIFERNO (MONTE), one of the highest summits in the Matese ridge of the Neapolitan Apennines.
BIGA, one of the small Shetland islands, between Mainland and Yell, in N lat. 60° 47', E long. 1° 30'.
BIGA. See BIGHA.
BIGADITZA, or BIGHADIJ, a village of Anatolia, in a fine plain in the valley of the Macestus, 4 hours N of Singerli.
BIGANOS, a commune of France, in the dep. of Gironde, cant. of Audenge, 24 m. SW of Bordeaux. Pop. 1,019.
BIGAR, an island in Mulgrave's archipelago, in the Pacific, the most northern of the Radack group.
BIG BEAR CREEK, a river of Upper Canada, a tributary of the St. Clair.
BIG BEAVER, a township in Beaver co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S. Pop. 1,329.
BIG BLACK, a river in the state of Mississippi, U. S., which falls into the Mississippi after a course of 160 m.
BIG BLUE, a river of N America, which rises in the Indian territory, and enters the Missouri in Jackson co.
BIG BONE CREEK, a river of Kentucky, which falls into the Ohio 40 m. below Cincinnati.
BIGBURY, a parish in Devonshire, 4 m. S of Modbury. Area 4,160 acres. Pop. 652.
BIGBURY BAY, a bay of the English channel, on the coast of Devonshire, between the Bolt-tail and Stoke point, into which the Erme and the Avon flow.
BIGBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 4 m. E of Glandford-bridge. Area 1,040 acres. Pop. 245. BIG

BIG CREEK, a township in Phillips co., in the state of Arkansas, U. S., 103 m. E of Little Rock. Pop. 392.—Also a township in Van Buren co., in Missouri. Pop. 1,106.—Also a township in Rives co., Missouri. Pop. 431.

BIG DRY, a river of North America, which runs into the Missouri, in about 49° N lat. Its channel is about half-a-mile wide, but, except when swelled by rains, or the melting of the mountain-snows, it contains only a few standing pools.
BIG EDDY, a river of Kentucky, which runs into the Cumberland.
BIG FLATS, a township in Chemung co., in the state of New York, U. S., on the line of the New York and Erie railroad, 204 m. SW of Albany. Pop. 1,375.

BIGGAR, a town and parish in the co. of Lanark. Area 5,852 Scots acres. Pop. of p. in 1801, 1,216; in 1841, 1,865, of whom 1,395 were in the town of B., which is 27 m. S by W of Edinburgh.—Biggar Water, a small stream which joins the Tweed a little below Drummelzier, has an elevation of 700 ft. in this p., and a descent of 25 ft., in a course of about 7 m. to the Tweed. When the head-stream of the Clyde is swollen by rain it discharges a portion of its waters eastwards into this stream.
BIGGE', an island of Nubia, adjacent to the island of Philæ, in the Nile, and containing some slight remains of antiquity.
BIGGE'S QUARTER, a township in the p. of Long Horsley, in Northumberland, 6 m. NNW of Morpeth. Pop. 252.

BIGGIN, a township in the p. of Wirksworth, in Derbyshire, 4 m. SW by S of Wirksworth. Pop. 149.—Also a township in Kirk-Fenton p. in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 6 m. WNW of Selby. Area 2,250 acres. Pop. 126.

BIGGLESWADE, a parish and market-town in the county of Bedford, on the river Ivel, on the great road from London to York, 101 m. ESE of Bedford, and 45 m. NNW of London. The river is navigable to this place; and joining the Ouse at Tempsford, opens a water-communication with Lynn Regis. The town has a weekly market on Wednesday, which was once one of the greatest in England for grain. Thread, lace, and straw plait are manufactured here. In 1785 a destructive fire destroyed about 120 dwelling houses besides store-houses and granaries, chiefly in the centre of the town and around the marketplace. On the 25th of February 1792, a smart shock of an earthquake was felt at B. The area of the p. is 4,220 acres. Pop., including that of the hamlets of Holme and Stratton, 3,807.

BIGHA, a sanjak and town of Turkey, in Anatolia. The sanjak is bounded on the W and S by the sea of Marmora, and embraces the Troas or Troad of the ancients.—The town—which occupies the site of the ancient Pegæ-stands on the border of a lake supposed by Rennell to be the ancient Aphnitis through which the Bigha, or Bighasui, the ancient Tarsius, flows towards the sea of Marmora.
BIG HATCHY, a river in the state of Tennessee, U. S., which, after a course of 100 m. falls into the Mississippi, in Tipton co.
BIG-HILL CREEK, in North America, runs west into Ruskaskias river, 26 m. N of its junction with the Mississippi.

BIG HORN, a river of North America, which rises in the Rocky mountains, near the Yellow Stone river, and the sources of the Platte; and finding its way through the eastern range of the Rocky mountains, and taking a northerly direction, falls into the Yellow Stone river at Mannel's fort, in about 47° N lat. In its long course it receives two considerable rivers,— one from the W, and the other from the S; and being unobstructed by falls, is navigable to a great distance in canoes, through a fine rich open country, well-supplied with timber.
BIGHT OF BENIN. See BENIN.
BIGHT OF BIAFRA. See BIAFRA.
BIGHTON, a parish in Hamps, 2 m. NE of Alresford. Area 1,580 acres. Pop. 284.
BIG ISLAND, an islet on the coast of Ireland, at the entrance of Carrickfergus bay, one of the Copeland group.
BIG ISLAND, a township in Marion co., in the state of Ohio, U. S., 47 m. W by N of Columbus. Pop. 554.
BIGLANDS, a township with Gamblesby, in the p. of Aikton, in Cumberland, 3 m. N of Wigton. Pop. 187.
BIGLEN, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Bern, bailiwick of Konolfingen. Pop. 2,977. There are mineral springs and baths here.
BIGNALL-END, a township in Audley p., in Staffordshire, 4 m. NW of Newcastle-under-Lyne. Pop. 517.
BIGNAN, a commune of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Saint-Jean-Brevelay. Pop. 2,822.
BIGNASCO, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Ticino, near the l. bank of the Maggia, 2 m. E of Cevio.
BIGNON (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Aigrefeuille. Pop. 1,909.
BIGNOR, a parish in Sussex, 5 m. S by E of Petworth. Area 1,180 acres. Pop. 210.
BIGNY-SUR-CHER, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Cher, com. of Vallenay, 9 m. NW of Saint- Pop. 230. There are large Amand - Montrond. iron-works here.
BIGONEA, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Orissa, 10 m. SW of Balasore.

BIGORRE, an ancient division of the province of Gascony, in France; bounded on the N by Armagnac; on the E by the Four valleys, Neborizan, and Astarac; on the S by the Pyrenees; and on the W by Bearn. It derived its name from the Bigerri, or Bigerrones, a tribe who inhabited the district in the time of Cæsar. It now forms a portion of the dep. of Hautes-Pyrénées. Area 242,000 hectares. .

BIGORRE (Pic du Midi de), a vast barren conical-shaped mountain of France, in the dep. of Hautes- Pyrénées. Reboul estimates its alt. at 9,538 ft. It is quite detached from the central range of the Pyrenees, and abuts at once upon the plain. From the Lac d'Oncet, "a little gem of dark blue set in the silver snow," the crest of the mountain shoots up in a cone of above 2,000 ft., covered with debris and disintegrated fragments of rock. It is composed of a brown micaceous schist, which, cropping out from the surface in masses, sparkles with metallic brilliancy. It commands one of the most extensive prospects in Europe over France; and embraces all the leading summits of the Hautes-Pyrénées.
BIGOTTIERE (LA), a commune and small town of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Chailland. Pop. 1,160.
BIG PELICAN ISLAND, a small arid island on the W part of the entrance of the bay of Mobile, 1 m. S of Dauphin island, on the coast of Florida, U.S.
BIG PRAIRIE, a township in New Madrid co., in the state of Missouri, U. S. Pop. 624.
BIG RIVER, a township in Jefferson co., in the state of Missouri, U.S., Pop. 965.
BIG ROCK BRANCH, the NE head branch of Alleghany river, in North America. The branch called Big Hole Town joins it, and forms the Alleghany.

BIG SANDY, a river of North America, which, formed by the junction of E and W Forks in Virgi- BIJ nia, separates the state of Virginia from that of Kentucky, and falls into the Ohio at Catletsburg in Kentucky. It is about 60 yards wide, and navigable 60 m. for loaded batteaux.—Little Sandy river falls into the Ohio, about 20 m. W of Big Sandy river.
BIG SPRINGS, a township in Seneca co., in the state of Ohio, U. S. Pop. 925.
BIGUACU, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Santa-Catharina, flowing into the sea a few leagues N of the embouchure of the Maruhi. It is navigable for large canoes a distance of 10 m.
BIGUGLIA, a commune and village of Corsica, in the cant. of Borgo, near a lagune of the same name, on the E coast. Pop. 264.
BIG WALNUT, an easterly branch of the Scioto, in the state of Ohio, U.S., 55 m. in length.
BIHAIN, a commune and village of Belgium, in Luxemburg, 30 m. NNE of Neufchateau. Pop. 860.
